nativeRange.habitat: Sheltered rocky reefs, intertidal reef flats, lagoon and seaward reefs; tends to occur around coral or over barren areas

diet.notes: Has been documented eating algae, hard-shelled invertebrates, small crustaceans, molluscs, polychaetes, tunicates, and even soft and stony corals in the wild. In captivity, generally advised to feed a varied meaty diet (squid, clams, krill, mysis, hard-shelled shrimp) interspersed with algae to wear down continuously growing beak-like teeth.

summary: A small Indo-Pacific sharpnose puffer (~10-12 cm) with dark netted markings and pale blue spots. Generally hardy and personable but not reef-safe, and carries tetrodotoxin in skin and organs.
careNotes: The Blue Spotted Puffer is one of the more manageable Canthigaster tobies, topping out around 10-12 cm and adaptable to mid-sized reef-grade systems (250 L+ generally advised given its constant cruising and territorial streak). It is best kept singly — conspecifics tend to fight, and mixing with other Canthigaster species is also commonly discouraged. Although marketed as 'reef-safe with caution,' field and aquarist accounts agree it has been known to nip soft corals, LPS polyps, feather dusters, ornamental shrimp, snails, and hermit crabs; treat it as not reef-safe for any prized invertebrate display. Like all tetraodontids, the species sequesters tetrodotoxin (TTX) in skin and organs; it can also release toxins into the water when severely stressed or dying, so handle gently — transfer in a container rather than a net to avoid air-puffing — and never house with anything you cannot afford to lose if a die-off event occurs. Diet should be varied and include hard-shelled foods (clams, krill, shrimp) to wear down its continuously growing fused beak. Source disagreement note: LiveAquaria recommends 50 gal (~190 L) minimum while reefapp.net recommends 400 L; chose 250 L as a cautious-leaning middle that respects the species' active cruising behaviour.
breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity. In the wild Canthigaster solandri is oviparous, facultatively monogamous, and adults are often found in pairs. No widely documented home-aquarium spawning protocol.

fish.breedingNotes: Pair-forming and oviparous in the wild; no established captive-breeding protocol.
